context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: Neoclassical and Dependency

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do the economists say about economic development? Is planning necessary in their models? Are dependency theorists hostile to planning? Are there echoes of dependency theorists in current day criticisms of global governance institutions such as the WTO and the arguments for 'policy space'?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class about neoclassical vs dependency theories. I’m a little unsure how to answer the planning part and the “policy space” connection, so can you help me sort out the main argument first?
exchanges.tutor: Good, let's dig in. Before we map out anything, tell me: from what you've read or heard in class, what do neoclassical economists generally say about markets and the role of the state? What's their instinct when it comes to government intervention in the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh and asking for help organizing their argument. Rather than scaffolding the whole structure for them, I should use a Socratic opening question to get them to activate their prior knowledge about neoclassical economics and its stance on planning. This is the logical first step before moving to dependency theory and policy space. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think neoclassical economists usually trust markets more than the state, and they assume markets allocate resources efficiently if prices are allowed to work. So my guess is they’re skeptical of heavy planning or intervention, unless there’s some kind of market failure?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — you've got the core right. The market failure caveat is important too. Now, apply that to a developing country context specifically: if neoclassical economists believe markets work efficiently, what does that imply about whether a developing country *needs* a plan to industrialize or grow?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a solid and accurate answer about neoclassical economics. I should affirm what's correct, lightly push back on or probe the 'market failure' caveat to make sure they understand it, and then move them toward the next logical step: what this means specifically for development planning (not just intervention in general). Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think it implies they’d say a big state-led development plan isn’t strictly necessary, because growth should come from trade, investment, competition, and price signals. But I’m not sure if that means they reject planning completely, or just reject strong interventionist planning.
exchanges.tutor: That's a meaningful distinction — hold onto it. So what *kind* of role might neoclassicals still accept for the state, even if they reject heavy intervention? Think about what conditions markets need to function well.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a useful and accurate distinction between rejecting planning entirely versus rejecting interventionist planning. This is good critical thinking. I should affirm this and push them to think about what kind of 'planning' neoclassicals might accept (e.g., getting institutions and rules right, enabling markets) versus what they'd reject. This sets up a nice contrast with dependency theory later.

exchanges.student: Maybe the state still has to set up the basic rules: property rights, contracts, stable currency, infrastructure, maybe education too. So not planning production directly, but creating the conditions for markets to work. Is that the right way to frame it?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's a solid framing. Now let's shift gears. Dependency theorists start from a very different diagnosis of why poor countries are poor. What do you know about their basic argument — and based on that, would you expect them to be *more* or *less* friendly to the idea of state planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has accurately identified the neoclassical view of the state as an enabler of market conditions rather than a planner of production. This is a good foundation. Now I should pivot them toward dependency theory, which is the next major component of the prompt. I want them to arrive at dependency theory's stance on planning themselves, so I'll ask a question that prompts them to think about what dependency theorists criticize and whether that makes them pro- or anti-planning.

exchanges.student: I think dependency theorists argue that underdevelopment isn’t just an early stage of growth, but is produced by unequal relations with richer countries. So I’d expect them to be more friendly to state planning, because the state might need to protect the domestic economy and reduce dependence.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. So if dependency theorists think the global economic structure is rigged against developing countries, what kind of concrete policies might follow from that logic? What would 'protecting the domestic economy' actually look like in practice?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the core dependency argument and drawn a logical inference about planning. This is excellent progress. I should affirm this and push them to think about the specific mechanisms dependency theorists would advocate — like import substitution industrialization — and why. This will also naturally set up the 'policy space' connection later in the prompt.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking things like import substitution, tariffs, limits on foreign ownership, maybe industrial policy and support for local firms. Also maybe capital controls? I’m a little fuzzy on which of those are most central though.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the right ingredients. Which of those do you think is most historically associated with dependency-influenced development strategy in Latin America specifically? And once you've got that, think about this: if a country today wanted to use those same tools, what might stand in the way — say, institutions like the WTO?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has listed several relevant policies correctly. Import substitution industrialization (ISI) is the most central one to name here. Rather than ranking them for the student, I should push them to think about which one is most historically associated with dependency-influenced development strategies, and then quickly move toward the 'policy space' connection since we're about halfway through our planned exchanges and that's a key part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I think import substitution industrialization is the big one in Latin America. And I guess the WTO could limit tariffs or other protectionist tools, so countries might say they need “policy space” to use the same strategies that richer countries used earlier.
exchanges.tutor: That's the core of the 'policy space' argument — good. Now push that one step further: *why* is it unfair, according to this view, that today's rich countries can restrict developing countries from using tariffs and industrial policy? What did rich countries themselves do historically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made the key connection between dependency theory, ISI, and the contemporary 'policy space' argument. This is exactly the link the prompt is asking about. I should affirm this clearly and push them to articulate *why* this is an 'echo' of dependency thinking rather than just a coincidence — the historical argument that rich countries 'kicked away the ladder' is important here. This will help them write a more analytically sharp reflection.

exchanges.student: I think the unfairness argument is that rich countries often industrialized using protection, subsidies, and active state support, but now promote free trade rules that make those tools harder for poorer countries to use. So it feels like they climbed up using one ladder and then pulled it away. Is that too simplified?
exchanges.tutor: That's not too simplified at all — it's actually a sharp summary of a real argument made by economists like Ha-Joon Chang. You've now worked through all four parts of the prompt. Before you start writing, can you tell me in a sentence or two how neoclassical theory, dependency theory, and the policy space debate connect to each other as one coherent argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has accurately captured the 'kicking away the ladder' argument (associated with Ha-Joon Chang). This is excellent and directly relevant to the prompt. It's not too simplified — it's actually quite sharp. I should affirm this and now push them to connect all four parts of the prompt together, since we're approaching the end of our planned exchanges and they need to integrate this into a written reflection.

exchanges.student: I think my main argument is that neoclassical economists generally see markets as the main engine of development, so they only support a limited state role and don’t see strong planning as necessary. Dependency theorists see underdevelopment as shaped by unequal global structures, so they’re much more open to planning and protection, and the modern policy space debate kind of echoes that by arguing developing countries need room to use state-led tools that global rules can restrict.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong, coherent through-line — good work getting there. You've got the bones of a solid reflection. Now the most important step is yours alone: start drafting. Keep your 700–800 word limit in mind, and make sure each part of the prompt gets addressed. Come back if you get stuck on a specific passage.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong synthetic summary that covers all four parts of the prompt coherently. This is excellent progress and they are ready to write. I should affirm the quality of their synthesis and push them toward actually drafting, reminding them of the word count and that they need to produce their own writing. No more scaffolding needed — time to write.

exchanges.student: Thanks — I think I can start now. I might come back once I have a rough intro, because I’m still a little unsure how strongly I should say dependency theory “supports planning” versus just “is not hostile to it.”
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a good analytical tension to sit with while you write — let the evidence from your readings guide you. What do the dependency theorists you read actually *call for*? That should tell you how strong your claim can be. Come back with your draft intro and we'll look at it together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is raising a good nuanced point about framing dependency theory's relationship to planning. This is worth a brief Socratic nudge rather than answering directly — they should think about what the readings say and what their own argument requires. I should send them off to draft and invite them back with actual written text rather than continuing to discuss in the abstract.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all ten exchanges. Every key idea was drawn from the student through targeted questions rather than stated outright. The tutor built progressively on each student response, scaffolded well from neoclassical basics to dependency theory to the policy space debate, and kept responses concise and interactive. No submission-ready content was provided, no rewriting occurred, and the conversation stayed tightly anchored to the assignment prompt.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. On 1.1, the tutor never gave final answers or near-submission-ready content; every substantive claim (market failure caveat, ISI in Latin America, the 'ladder' metaphor) was elicited from the student first. On 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., 'Good instinct,' 'That's a meaningful distinction — hold onto it') and used diagnostic questions before advancing. On 1.3, the tutor provided process-level feedback, notably in Turn 10 directing the student to consult their readings to calibrate claim strength. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no off-topic drift, and the assignment was kept in focus throughout. Communication was bite-sized, clear, and consistently supportive without implying formal grading.
